plan.contextChunk:
Now that we have seen how 3D to 2D projection can be effectively modeled
using homogeneous coordinates and simple vector computations, let's
apply these tools to model cameras.

We want to construct a camera model that captures the image formation
process. Cameras translate the world into pixels, but the relationship
between the 3D scene and the 2D image captured by the camera is not
always straightforward. To start with, the world is measured in units of
meters while points in images are measured in pixels. Factors such as
geometric distortions can influence this relationship, and we need to
account for these elements in our camera model.

### From Meters to Pixels

@fig-pinhole_and_sensor illustrates the image formation process and the
image sampling by the sensor. We need to account for the perspective
projection of the point, $\mathbf{P}$, described in camera coordinates,
to pixel coordinates in the sensor plane of the camera.

![An image is projected into the sensor. World coordinates are transformed into pixels at the sensor. The focal length is $f$, and the physical width of the sensor is $w$. The sensor has $N \times M$ pixels.](figures/imaging_geometry/pinhole_and_sensor.png){width="100%" #fig-pinhole_and_sensor}

The units of the position in pixel space may be different than those of
the world coordinates, which scales the focal length, $f$, in equation
(@eq-homogPerspective) to some other value. Let's call that value $a$:
$$\mathbf{K} =    \begin{bmatrix}
    a & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
    0 & a & 0 & 0 \\
    0 & 0 & 1 & 0
    \end{bmatrix}
    $${#eq-intrinsic} where the constant $a$ is related to the physical
camera parameters in the following way: $$a = f \, N / w$$ where $f$ isthe focal length (in meters), $w$ is the width of the sensor in meters,
and $N$ is the image width in pixels. The ratio $N/w$ is the number of
pixels per meter in the sensor.

Also, the image center might not be the pixel $[0,0]^\mathsf{T}$. If the

elements: camera center point (black sphere at origin)
optical axis / principal axis (Z_c line stretching from sensor to house)
3D coordinate axes at origin: Y_c (up), X_c (pointing diagonally forward-left), Z_c (right along optical axis)
virtual image plane (translucent gray rectangle at +f, with green axes x and y)
sensor image plane (translucent grid at -f, with dimensions labeled M pixels x N pixels, and physical width w)
3D house model (green facade, yellow windows, red roof, located on the right side of the scene)
point P (cyan sphere on the corner of the house)
projected point on virtual image plane (cyan sphere)
projected point on sensor plane (cyan sphere, inverted)
projection ray (dashed line passing from point P, through the camera center, to the sensor plane point)
pixelated projection preview overlaid on the sensor grid (inverted, colored yellow/green/red to mimic the house)
dimension lines with double-headed arrows for focal length f and sensor width w

interactions.label: Focal Length (f)
interactions.teaches: Moving the planes further from the pinhole increases the projection size, demonstrating how focal length directly controls magnification (zoom).

interactions.label: Sensor Pixels (N)
interactions.teaches: Increasing the number of pixels N over the same physical width w makes the sampling grid finer, capturing a higher resolution image.

demo_steps.title: The Pinhole Projection Geometry
demo_steps.narration: Welcome to the 3D pinhole camera model. Light rays from a 3D point P on the house pass straight through the camera center (pinhole) at the origin. They project onto the physical sensor plane at distance -f behind the camera, creating an inverted, upside-down image.

demo_steps.title: Virtual vs. Real Image Plane
demo_steps.narration: To make the math simpler and avoid dealing with upside-down images, we often define a 'virtual' image plane at distance +f in front of the camera. The geometry is identical, but the virtual image is upright and directly matches our forward view.

demo_steps.title: Increasing Focal Length (Zooming)
demo_steps.narration: Watch the projection scale as we increase focal length f to 2.8. Both planes move further from the pinhole, pushing the projected points outward. This behaves like a zoom lens, magnifying the target point P on our sensor.

demo_steps.title: Discretizing into Pixels
demo_steps.narration: Now look closely at the sensor grid. Increasing the pixel count N divides the physical sensor width w into more samples. This increases pixel density, allowing the digital sensor to resolve fine spatial details of the projected house.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Slightly elevated perspective looking from the front-right towards the camera center, showing the full chain of projection from sensor, through origin, to the house.
interactionPlan.view_reasoning: (1) The dominant baseline is the optical axis Z_c which runs horizontally left-to-right across the page. (2) This left-right baseline implies the camera is placed broadside to the optical axis, but tilted slightly forward to reveal the depth separation between the planes and house (azimuth around 25 degrees). (3) The top edges of the image planes are visible, showing a downward-tilted perspective (elevation around 15 degrees).

camera_view.view_notes: Maintains the classic textbook diagram composition: sensor grid is clearly visible on the left, camera center at center-left, virtual plane in the center, and the 3D house on the right.

inline_constraints.default_view: must match the original image silhouette, size, label scale, and crop
inline_constraints.visible_ui: at most 2 compact sliders near the bottom/right edge, with no filled panel backing
inline_constraints.explainers: hover tooltips and click popups only
inline_constraints.framing: match the source crop and whitespace
inline_constraints.camera: infer the source camera angle so the first rendered frame is a seamless visual replacement

interactionPlan.notes: Render the pixel grid on the sensor plane dynamically using lines. Color the cells behind the projected house with a low-res pixelated texture matching the house's colors (red/yellow/green) to visually connect the continuous house geometry to the discrete sampled digital image. On changing focalLength, dynamically translate both planes and stretch the projection lines so they remain perfectly aligned.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure shows a camera projection model with the camera center at the origin, a sensor plane on the left, a virtual image plane in the center-right, and a 3D house on the far right. The viewpoint is orthographic, slightly elevated and looking from the front-left toward the back-right, making all planes and the house clearly separated and legible.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot captures the same spatial layout but is extremely zoomed out, making all elements appear clustered and tiny in the center of the frame. The perspective angle matches the original, but the scale and framing are vastly different.
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: While the directional viewing angle is largely correct, the camera is far too distant (under-zoomed), leaving excessive whitespace and compressing the visual details of the figure.

shape_comparison.rendered_shape: flat 2D plates with gray line-drawn wireframe depth

discrepancies: The figure is heavily under-zoomed, making all components look tiny and hard to read.
The house's depth is rendered with thin gray wireframe lines rather than solid 3D colored volumes (the side wall should be green and the side roof red).
Labels like 'Optical / principal axis' and 'Z_c' overlap due to the cramped layout.
The pixelated preview cells on the sensor plane do not perfectly match the clean look of the pixel grid projection on the left of the original figure.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive concept modeling, but the final camera view is too zoomed out, and the house lacks solid 3D depth geometry.
action_items: Adjust the camera zoom in setCameraView to frame the elements closely, mimicking the crop and scale of the original figure.
Rebuild the house depth as solid 3D meshes (solid green side wall and solid red roof) instead of using gray lines.
Increase the font size of the labels and adjust their positions to prevent overlaps (especially near the coordinate axes).
Thicken the projection ray dashed line and make the projected cyan dots larger so they stand out at proper scale.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual model and interactivity are excellent, but the camera zoom is off and the house depth geometry needs to be rendered as solid 3D meshes rather than wireframes, which are implementation-level adjustments.
action_items: Adjust the camera zoom in setCameraView to frame the elements closely, mimicking the crop and scale of the original figure.
Rebuild the house depth as solid 3D meshes (solid green side wall and solid red roof) instead of using gray lines.
Increase the font size of the labels and adjust their positions to prevent overlaps (especially near the coordinate axes).
Thicken the projection ray dashed line and make the projected cyan dots larger so they stand out at proper scale.
